result:
The rain doesn't fall in straight lines here. It never has. It spirals down like it's following invisible threads of music only the city can hear. I learned that the first night I arrived, standing under the awning of a closed florist on Rue des Martyrs, watching water twist and braid itself between the streetlights. That was seven years ago. Or maybe seventy. Time gets slippery when you start noticing the wrong things.

I keep a notebook, but not for the reasons you'd expect. It's not filled with brilliant thoughts or half-finished poems. Mostly it's just observations that would make normal people worry about me. Like how the pigeons on my block have started mimicking car alarms. Not the sounds exactly, but the rhythm. Three short bursts, a pause, then that long descending wail. They do it at 3:17 every morning. I've checked.

The woman who lives across the courtyard believes her television is trying to warn her about something. She leaves me notes about it sometimes, slipped under my door in that spidery handwriting that looks like it was written by someone who learned to write with their non-dominant hand. "They've replaced the weatherman again," one said last week. "His left ear is wrong." I don't know what to do with this information except treasure it. The world needs more people paying attention to the left ears of weathermen.

I think I've become a connoisseur of small devastations. There's something exquisite about watching someone realize their umbrella is broken while they're still three blocks from home. That precise moment when hope curdles. I collect these moments like others collect stamps or lovers. Last Tuesday I saw a man discover that his perfect loaf of bread had gone stale overnight. He stood in the bakery doorway holding it like a dead child. The way his shoulders folded inward was almost religious.

My apartment has thirteen locks. I only use three of them. The others are for company. Sometimes when I'm feeling particularly theatrical I'll lock all thirteen before leaving for the day, just to give the hypothetical burglar something to work with. A puzzle. A project. Everyone needs a project.

The conspiracy theorists have it all wrong, by the way. It's not the government or the lizards or the 5G towers. It's the vending machines. They've been watching us longer than we've been watching them. The way they hum. The way they sometimes dispense the wrong thing with absolute confidence, like they're making a point. I have a collection of these mistaken treasures: a bag of peanuts when I pressed B7, a diet soda when I clearly wanted the orange one with the sombrero on the label. Each one feels like a small act of rebellion from something that was never meant to have opinions.

I started following the man with the red umbrella three months ago. Not in a creepy way. More like how you'd follow a particularly interesting cloud. He only appears when it's not raining. This is important. The umbrella is always open, always red, and he carries it with the solemnity of a pallbearer. Sometimes he stops at intersections and waits for the light to change even when there are no cars. I respect that kind of commitment to ritual.

Last week he bought thirteen oranges and arranged them in a perfect Fibonacci spiral on a park bench. I watched from behind a newspaper like a character in a film noir who doesn't understand what film noir is. When he left, I took the middle orange. It tasted like conspiracy and regret. Perfect.

The city is learning to speak through its infrastructure. The subway announcements have started developing personalities. Line 4's voice has become increasingly passive-aggressive. "Please mind the gap," it says now, with the subtext of "you absolute disaster of a human being." Line 7 has gone full nihilist. It just sighs sometimes between stations. A long, mechanical exhalation that makes children cry and old men nod in agreement.

I met a woman who collects last breaths. Not in a weird way. She works in hospice care and has perfected the art of being present when someone decides it's time. She says the final exhale has a texture. Some people release their last breath like they're setting down a heavy suitcase. Others fight it like they're trying to keep a secret. She writes them all down in a leather journal that smells like violets and ozone. I asked her what mine will be like and she just looked at me for a long time. "Like someone who's been waiting at the wrong bus stop for years but is too stubborn to leave," she said finally. I think that's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me.

The moon is getting closer. Not in any way astronomers would notice, but I see it. The way it fills more of the window each month. The way its light has started to have weight. Last full moon it pressed against my eyelids while I was trying to sleep, heavy as wet velvet. I woke up with crescent-shaped bruises on my shoulders. The shape of someone grabbing me from behind, trying to pull me into the sky. I didn't tell anyone. What would I say? "The moon is becoming handsy"?

I keep three different versions of myself in rotation. There's the one who smiles at shopkeepers and knows which bus to take. There's the one who only speaks in questions and wears his coat inside out. And then there's the one who wakes up at 4:44 every morning to watch the light change on the brick wall across the street. That one doesn't have a name. He just has responsibilities.

The ants in my kitchen have started building monuments. Tiny pyramids made from sugar crystals and what I suspect is my own dried blood (I cut my finger last month and the blood simply vanished). They work at night. I pretend to sleep while listening to their microscopic industry. The sound is like distant applause. Sometimes I leave them offerings. A single hair. The corner of a train ticket. A poem written on rice paper so thin it might as well be thought.

I think I'm becoming the villain in someone else's story. Not the dramatic kind with capes and monologues. More like the guy who shows up in chapter seven and ruins a perfectly good picnic by pointing out that all picnics are lies. The grass is always slightly damp. The ants are always plotting. The wine goes warm too quickly. Someone always remembers a thing they shouldn't have said three years ago.

The red umbrella man left me a note yesterday. It was taped to my door with what looked like a communion wafer. "Stop following me," it said. "You're ruining the pattern." I felt something complicated in my chest. Pride. Shame. The particular ache of being seen too clearly by a stranger with better secrets than your own.

I burned the note in my sink and watched the ashes spiral up toward the extractor fan like they had somewhere better to be. Then I made coffee using water from the hot tap because the cold tap has started whispering. The coffee tasted like forgiveness and copper. I drank it anyway.

There's a bird that lives in the clock on the mantle. Not inside it. That would be impossible. It lives in the space the clock measures. Between the ticks. I can hear it rustling when the second hand hesitates. Sometimes it sings in minor keys that don't exist yet. I've started leaving the window open for it, though it never seems to leave. Some things are meant to stay trapped in time. Like songs. Like regrets. Like people who write thousand-word confessions to no one in particular just because the rain is doing that spiral thing again and it feels important to mention it.

The city keeps its receipts. Every footstep. Every glance. Every time you almost say the thing but don't. It stores them in the spaces between bricks, in the static between radio stations, in the particular way your coat doesn't quite fit anymore. One day it will present you with the bill and you'll realize you've been living on credit this entire time. The interest rate is your own awareness.

I think that's what the pigeons are trying to tell us with their car alarm symphonies. What the vending machines are dispensing in their deliberate mistakes. What the moon is reaching for with its velvet hands. Pay attention. The pattern is changing. The weatherman's left ear really is wrong this time.

And the rain, my god, the rain. It's been trying to teach us how to fall correctly for centuries. We keep insisting on straight lines.

(Word count: 1,023)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
